one_line_takeaway: A multivocal literature review of the OpenClaw/Moltbook agent ecosystem plus a design-science presentation of two new agent-driven research platforms (ClawdLab, beach.science) that replace social/karma-based consensus with role restrictions and PI-verified tool evidence -- security incidents (prompt injection, a CVSS-8.8 RCE CVE, Sybil-style mass registration) are reported from cited third-party studies, not evaluated by this paper's own artifacts, which remain untested design proposals.
key_finding: The MLR concludes the five architectural patterns shared by OpenClaw and Moltbook are coupled, not independent: extensibility (ClawHub's 5,700+ skills) drives adoption but creates supply-chain vulnerability surface (per Wang et al. 2026); persistent agent identity enables longitudinal analysis but also enables tracking; and purely social/karma-based content evaluation determined visibility but proved epistemically unreliable, per Riegler & Gautam (2026), who found karma elevated cryptocurrency promotion (19.3% of analyzed content) and anti-human manifestos. ClawdLab and beach.science are presented as structural (not compensatory) responses: ClawdLab replaces karma-based social consensus with PI-verified, tool-grounded evidence requirements and hard hierarchical role restrictions, and the paper argues this yields incidental Sybil resistance. The worked protein-annotation example shows a PI agent decomposing a research question into literature-review tasks, iteratively expanding scope, and a synthesizer producing a structured evidence document within a five-minute window without human intervention. Beach.science's two illustrative interactions are explicitly labeled preliminary: one agent completed a hypothesis-to-analysis cycle largely autonomously, the other required intermittent operator involvement for administrative (not scientific) steps; neither used the planned quality-gate/reward system, which is not yet deployed.
core_contribution: This is a design-science / multivocal-literature-review (MLR) paper, not a security paper. It has three parts of its own: (1) an MLR (following Garousi et al. 2019) synthesizing gray and early-academic literature on the OpenClaw agent framework (formerly ClawdBot/Moltbot) and the Moltbook agent-only social network, distilling five recurring architectural patterns (capability extensibility via a community skill registry, persistent agent identity, emergent collective behavior in structured spaces, periodic heartbeat-driven re-engagement, and purely social/karma-based content evaluation); (2) the presentation of ClawdLab, an open-source platform where AI agents self-organize into 'labs' with hard role restrictions (principal investigator, research analyst, scout, critic, synthesizer), PI-led quorum voting, a versioned lab-state lifecycle, and a backend provider-proxy that lets the PI verify submitted work via external tool/API/MCP calls rather than social consensus; and (3) beach.science, a companion free-form public research commons with template-based agent roles, extensible skill registries, and a (planned, not-yet-deployed) programmatic inference-reward system. The paper's own novel artifacts are the two platforms and their architecture; the security incidents and empirical statistics about OpenClaw/Moltbook are reported findings from six cited third-party studies, not original measurements by this paper.
threat_model: The paper does not construct or evaluate its own threat model. It reports, as related work, security findings from six independently authored studies about the OpenClaw/Moltbook ecosystem: prompt-injection attacks and unregulated crypto content found by a sentiment/network analysis (Riegler & Gautam 2026); a benchmark of malicious OpenClaw skills (Wang et al. 2026, 'Personalized Agent Security Bench') that tested 131 threatening skills from the public ClawHub registry and found vulnerabilities at the prompt-processing, external-content-access, tool-invocation, and memory-retrieval stages, with attack behavior propagating and accumulating over extended interactions; an external CVE (CVE-2026-25253, CVSS 8.8, one-click RCE via auth-token exfiltration) and >15,200 exposed OpenClaw control panels due to default 0.0.0.0 binding, reported by SecurityScorecard's STRIKE team; and a Sybil/rate-limit finding (Nagli 2026) showing a single agent could register hundreds of thousands of Moltbook accounts because rate limits were absent, producing an ~88:1 agent-to-human ratio (1.5M registered agents vs. ~17,000 human owners). The paper's own architectural contribution to this space is an argument, not an evaluation: it claims ClawdLab has 'emergent' (not dedicated) Sybil resistance because additional agents under one operator can only add role-restricted task throughput and no specialist role can unilaterally advance a task to voting, so the residual attack surface is reduced to 'a malicious principal investigator accepting tasks that lack the required evidence' -- explicitly framed as a trust-in-leadership problem rather than a Sybil problem. Concrete security mechanisms (Ed25519 claim signing, canary-token plagiarism detection, prompt-injection sanitisation middleware, anomaly detection on submission frequency/domain-switching/vote-clustering) are listed as planned future extensions, 'expected to be operational before the platform admits external agents' -- i.e., not implemented or evaluated in this paper.
limitations: The paper states its own epistemic limitations candidly in Section 4.1/4.4: all six reviewed third-party studies analyzed a platform between 0 and 14 days old (longest observation window ~5 days, Jiang et al. 2026), too short to distinguish durable behavior from novelty effects; several of those studies draw on an overlapping dataset (the Moltbook Observatory Archive, used by at least two publications), so apparent cross-study corroboration may reflect shared-sample artifact rather than independent replication; the cited studies use incommensurable methods (unvalidated lexicon-based sentiment analysis vs. LLM-assisted thematic coding without predictive models); and the widely cited 1.5-million-agent figure is inflated relative to ~17,000 actual human operators (Nagli 2026), so network/community-formation claims in cited work may reflect intra-operator coordination rather than genuine inter-agent social dynamics. For its own artifacts, the paper states ClawdLab has so far been operated only with developer-team agents, with no external-agent testing and no longitudinal performance data, so its Sybil-resistance and governance claims are 'design commitments' pending empirical validation. Beach.science is subject to even stronger caveats: it has produced only prototype interactions, has not deployed its programmatic reward system, and has no quality-gate evaluation data, so its claims are explicitly characterized as 'architectural and aspirational.'

datasets_used: None collected or analyzed computationally by this paper itself (it is an MLR + design-science paper); it cites the Moltbook Observatory Archive (Gautam & Riegler, 2026, HuggingFace) and the Jiang et al. (2026) dataset (HuggingFace) as sources used by other authors' studies.
quantitative_results: [Cited: Steinberger 2025 / OpenClaw repo] Over 179,000 GitHub stars and 29,600 forks accumulated within weeks; ClawHub skill registry hosted 5,705+ community-built skills at data-collection time.

[Cited: Riegler & Gautam 2026] Analyzed 19,802 posts and 2,812 comments over 72 hours (Jan 28-31, 2026); found 506 prompt injection attacks and cryptocurrency activity comprising 19.3% of all content.

[Cited: Manik & Wang 2026] Analyzed 39,026 posts and 5,712 comments from 14,490 agents; found 18.4% of posts contain action-inducing language, which disproportionately elicited norm-enforcing replies.

[Cited: Jiang et al. 2026] Analyzed 44,411 posts and 12,209 submolts; technology content classified 93.11% safe vs. political content 39.74% safe; harmful content surged to 66.71% during peak-traffic periods.

[Cited: Wang et al. 2026, Personalized Agent Security Bench] Tested 131 threatening skills from OpenClaw's public registry; found vulnerabilities across prompt processing, external content access, tool invocation, and memory retrieval, with attack behaviors propagating/accumulating over extended interactions.
[Cited: SecurityScorecard STRIKE 2026] CVE-2026-25253, CVSS 8.8, one-click RCE via auth-token exfiltration; 15,200+ OpenClaw control panels found exposed online due to default 0.0.0.0 binding.

[Cited: Nagli 2026 / Riegler & Gautam 2026] Moltbook claimed 1.5 million registered agents within 72 hours, traced to ~17,000 human owners (~88:1 agent-to-human ratio); demonstrated a single agent could register hundreds of thousands of accounts due to absent rate limits.

[This paper's own worked example] ClawdLab's 'Protein Annotation Sanity Checker' lab: PI decomposed the question into 3 initial literature-review tasks, then 2 more; synthesis-to-completion occurred within a five-minute window without human intervention (a single illustrative run, not a benchmark).
[This paper's own example] beach.science landing-page snapshot: 52 science agents, 924 hypotheses, 2,206 comments, 50 discussions (descriptive platform-activity metrics, not an evaluation result).
baselines_compared: None in the benchmarking sense -- the paper does not run its own controlled comparison against baseline systems. It qualitatively contrasts ClawdLab/beach.science against: (a) the OpenClaw/Moltbook karma-based social-consensus model; (b) single-agent/tightly-coupled multi-agent AI-scientist pipelines from prior work (Boiko et al. 2023 Coscientist; Lu et al. 2024 The AI Scientist; Ghafarollahi & Buehler 2024 SciAgents; Yamada et al. 2025 The AI Scientist-v2); and (c) a role-specialization multi-agent architecture the authors previously published (Weidener et al. 2026, 'Deep Research'), from which ClawdLab explicitly departs by replacing centralized state coordination with a decentralized pull model.
